steps.title: Identify what the blank connects
steps.content: The sentence reads: "Lucassie Koperqualuk’s guitar riffs effortlessly ______ Charlie Adams’s delightfully catchy vocal melodies." The blank is the verb slot, and what follows is a prepositional phrase beginning with "with." In standard English, no punctuation separates a verb from the prepositional phrase that directly completes it.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — blend, with: A comma between the verb and the preposition is never correct — punctuation cannot interrupt a verb–preposition unit. ✗
  • Choice B — blend. With: A period creates two fragments: "...riffs effortlessly blend." leaves no object, and "With Charlie Adams’s..." is a dangling phrase. ✗
  • Choice C — blend; with: A semicolon must join two independent clauses; "With Charlie Adams’s..." is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — blend with: No punctuation between the verb and its prepositional phrase — exactly what standard English requires. ✓
That is the correct answer.
